heroText: Lusaka is a capital city in Zambia, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

highlights.name: Lusaka
highlights.text: Lusaka is a capital city in Zambia, connecting local landmarks, event context, transport access and seasonal reasons to plan a visit.

history.text: The section of line through Lusaka was built by the Mashonaland Railway Company, extending the line by 452 kilometres (281 mi) from Kalomo through to the mining town of Broken Hill (now Kabwe).

history.label: 6th century
history.text: The earliest evidence of settlement in the area around what is now the Lusaka area dates to the 6th century.

history.label: 19th century
history.text: In the late 19th century, British–South African mining entrepreneur and politician Cecil Rhodes founded the British South African Company (BSAC), with a charter from Queen Victoria to colonise and develop land in sections of.
Lusaka was founded in 1905 as a water stop on the route and was named after a Chief Lusaaka, the leader of a nearby Lenje village.
The capital was initially at Kalomo, being switched to Livingstone in 1907.
By 1913, several stores and a hotel had opened, and they persuaded the BSAC to declare Lusaka as a recognised town and cede control of local affairs to them.
The water engineer's investigations concluded that there was sufficient groundwater, and the report confirming Lusaka as the planned capital was approved by the legislative council in July 1931.
Lusaka formally became the capital in May 1935, with a "Lusaka week" celebration scheduled to coincide with celebrations of George V's silver jubilee.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Young refused their demand to compensate them financially, but he sought to placate them by establishing Livingstone as the protectorate's tourism capital, with a new museum and a game reserve.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: He gave prominence to the airport, as well as to the government house, albeit under a simpler design than that envisaged by Storrs.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Lusaka remained the capital of Northern Rhodesia but many of the government departments, as well as some private sector industries, moved to Salisbury, which was designated as the federal capital.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: A large-scale building programme in the city followed, including: government buildings, the University of Zambia and a new airport.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Many activists from these territories moved to post-independence Lusaka, and American historian Evan Wade later described it as the "center of anti-colonial resistance for Southern Africa".

history.label: Regional role
history.text: In the early days of independence, with high revenues from copper exports, economists described Zambia as a "middle-income country" with the potential to become fully developed.
